questions.question: Which of the following is/are correct about C. Vijayaraghavachariar?I. He was called ‘The Hero of Salem’.II. He was known as the ‘Lion of North India’.
questions.solution: C. Vijayaraghavachariar was a prominent leader of the Indian National Congress. He was known as the 'Hero of Salem' due to his involvement in the Salem Riots case of 1882, where he successfully defended himself against false charges. The title 'Lion of the Punjab' (not North India) belonged to Lala Lajpat Rai, making statement II incorrect.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are correct about Tej Bahadur Sapru?I. He helped Annie Besant to build up the Central Hindu College at Banaras. II. He entered politics during the Home Rule movement. III. He participated in the Round Table Conferences held between 1930 and 1932 in London.
questions.solution:
Tej Bahadur Sapru was an eminent lawyer and leader of the Liberal Party. His contributions include:

  • Education: He assisted Annie Besant in the development of the Central Hindu College at Banaras.
  • Home Rule: He entered active politics during the Home Rule movement (1916).
  • Round Table Conferences: He played a crucial role as a mediator and participated in all three Round Table Conferences (1930–1932) to discuss constitutional reforms for India.

questions.question: According to Dadabhai Naoroji’s Drain of Wealth theory, what major economic issue needed to be addressed to end India’s exploitation under British rule?
questions.option_1: Excessive land revenue imposed on peasants
questions.option_3: Unequal economic exchange between India and Britain
questions.solution: Dadabhai Naoroji, in his book 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India', formulated the Drain of Wealth theory. He argued that Britain was unilaterally transferring India's resources and wealth to England without any corresponding economic return. To end this exploitation, he advocated for a stop to this unequal economic exchange and the 'moral drain' of Indian talent.

questions.question: How did Mahatma Gandhi’s views on untouchability shape the priorities of the Indian National Congress during the Gandhian phase?
questions.option_1: Endorsing separate political electorates for communities
questions.option_2: Making abolition of untouchability a central objective
questions.option_3: Concentrating on political integration of princely states
questions.option_4: Shifting primary focus toward agrarian economic reforms
questions.solution: During the Gandhian phase, the Indian National Congress underwent a massive ideological shift. Gandhi considered untouchability a 'blot on Hinduism.' He made its abolition a central objective of the Congress program, integrating social reform with the political struggle for Swaraj and renaming the affected communities as 'Harijans' (Children of God).

questions.question: Prafulla Chaki and Khudiram Bose are known for their attempt to assassinate which British official?​
questions.solution: In 1908, young revolutionaries Prafulla Chaki and Khudiram Bose threw a bomb at a carriage in Muzaffarpur. Their target was Douglas Kingsford, a British Magistrate known for passing harsh sentences against nationalist activists. However, the attempt missed Kingsford and unfortunately killed two British women, Mrs. and Miss Pringle Kennedy.

questions.question: The Indian Home Rule League at Madras was founded by ______.
questions.solution: The Home Rule League was launched in 1916 through two separate organizations. While Bal Gangadhar Tilak started his league in Belgaum (covering Maharashtra and Karnataka), Annie Besant founded the Indian Home Rule League at Madras, which eventually established branches across the rest of India.

questions.question: What key difference set the Kheda Satyagraha apart from the Ahmedabad Mill Strike during the phase of heightened nationalist activity?
questions.option_1: Reliance on petitions and appeals to colonial authorities
questions.option_2: Common leadership under the same nationalist figures
questions.option_3: Emphasis on constitutional and legislative demands
questions.option_4: Mobilisation of rural peasants versus organisation of urban workers
questions.solution:
In 1918, Mahatma Gandhi led two distinct movements in Gujarat that showcased different social bases:

  • Kheda Satyagraha: Focused on rural peasants who were unable to pay land revenue due to crop failure.
  • Ahmedabad Mill Strike: Focused on the urban industrial workers (mill hands) regarding a dispute over 'Plague Bonuses' and wage hikes.

questions.question: What was a consequence of the economic structure in India between 1947 and 1991?
questions.option_1: High levels of foreign direct investment
questions.option_2: Full privatization of the public sector
questions.option_3: Slow industrial growth due to heavy regulation and protectionism
questions.option_4: Rapid technological advancement in all sectors
questions.solution: The Indian economic structure between 1947 and 1991 followed a 'Mixed Economy' model with a heavy socialist bias. This era was characterized by the 'License-Permit Raj,' high protectionism, and heavy regulation. A major consequence was slow industrial growth (often called the 'Hindu Rate of Growth') and limited technological modernization due to a lack of global competition.

questions.question: What was the immediate cause that sparked the 1857 uprising among Indian sepoys?
questions.option_2: Use of greased cartridges in Enfield rifles
questions.option_4: Attack on Lucknow by the British army
questions.solution: The 1857 Uprising had many deep-seated causes, but the immediate spark was the introduction of the Enfield Rifle. The cartridges for these rifles were rumored to be greased with cow and pig fat, which had to be bitten off before use. This deeply offended the religious sentiments of both Hindu and Muslim sepoys, leading to the mutiny at Meerut.

questions.question: What was the name of the headquarters of the Ghadar Party?
questions.solution: The Ghadar Party was an international revolutionary movement founded by Indians in North America. Their headquarters in San Francisco was named the Yugantar Ashram. From here, they published the weekly journal 'Ghadar' to incite a rebellion against British rule in India.

questions.question: Which demand advanced by the Moderate leadership of the Indian National Congress (1885–1905) most clearly reflected their objective of expanding Indian involvement within colonial decision-making structures?
questions.option_2: Preferential allocation of bureaucratic appointments to Indians
questions.option_3: Expansion of legislative councils for Indian representation
questions.option_4: Immediate transfer of political authority to Indian hands
questions.solution: The Moderates believed in 'constitutional agitation' and gradual reforms. Their primary objective regarding governance was the expansion of Legislative Councils and increasing the number of Indian representatives within them. This was partially realized through the Indian Councils Act of 1892.

questions.question: Which form of nationalist protest emerged in response to the Partition of Bengal in 1905 that integrated economic, social, and political action?
questions.option_1: Boycotting British goods and promoting indigenous industries
questions.option_2: Submitting petitions to the British Parliament for reversal
questions.option_3: Organising armed resistance against colonial authorities
questions.option_4: Pursuing constitutional agitation within legislative councils
questions.solution: The Swadeshi and Boycott Movement emerged in 1905 as a direct response to Lord Curzon's partition of Bengal. It was a multifaceted protest that encouraged the boycott of British-made goods (especially cloth) and the promotion of indigenous (Swadeshi) industries, schools, and cultural institutions.

questions.question: Which individuals actively supported Mahatma Gandhi during the Kheda Satyagraha movement?
questions.option_4: Raj Kumar Shukla and Rajendra Prasad
questions.solution: In the Kheda Satyagraha (1918), Gandhi was actively supported by local leaders who went from village to village to organize the peasants. His most prominent lieutenants during this struggle were Vallabhbhai Patel (who gave up his legal practice) and Indulal Yajnik. Ambalal Sarabhai and Anasuya Ben were associated with the Ahmedabad Mill Strike.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT true regarding the Poona Pact of 1932?
questions.option_1: It dropped the idea of separate electorates for depressed classes.
questions.option_2: It was accepted by the government.
questions.option_3: Seats reserved for depressed classes increased in the legislatures.
questions.option_4: Ambedkar refused to sign the pact.
questions.solution:
The Poona Pact (1932) was an agreement between Dr. B.R. Ambedkar and Mahatma Gandhi. It is true that:

  • It dropped the idea of separate electorates for the depressed classes in favor of joint electorates.
  • The number of reserved seats for the depressed classes in provincial legislatures was increased from 71 to 148.
  • Ambedkar did sign the pact, making the statement that he refused to sign it incorrect.

questions.question: Which reformer attempted to synthesize Indian and Western religious thought in the 19th century?
questions.solution: Raja Ram Mohan Roy, often called the 'Father of Indian Renaissance,' was a pioneer in synthesizing Indian and Western thought. He studied the Upanishads and the Bible, advocating for a monotheistic form of Hinduism (Brahmo Samaj) that adopted Western rationalism and humanism while remaining rooted in Indian spiritual traditions.
